artifacts.description: Run log documenting why the requested empirical analysis could not be executed honestly with available and retrievable data in this run.

limitations: No usable microdata source was successfully materialized in this run that jointly measured e-cigarette use and epilepsy/seizure history for women under age 50.

The official CDC 2015 BRFSS documentation page was accessible, but text search of the accessible 2015 combined codebook did not surface e-cigarette, vaping, epilepsy, or seizure variables in the inspected file.
Python-based direct download of the BRFSS 2015 transport archive failed because of network name-resolution failure inside the container, preventing local computation.
Because no verified analytic dataset was obtained, no original prevalence estimate, confidence interval, or association model could be produced honestly.
methods: I translated the idea into a cross-sectional observational question and evaluated the provided public-data options for a feasible empirical analysis. I searched for a retrievable dataset containing both e-cigarette use and epilepsy/seizure history, prioritizing BRFSS 2015 because it was suggested in the dataset hints. I opened the official CDC 2015 BRFSS survey-data page, inspected its documentation, opened the official 2015 BRFSS codebook PDF, and searched the codebook text for terms related to e-cigarettes, vaping, epilepsy, and seizures. The accessible codebook view did not reveal matching variables for e-cigarette use or epilepsy/seizure history in the inspected file. I then attempted direct data retrieval in Python from the CDC 2015 BRFSS transport archive to run an original weighted analysis, but the container could not resolve the external host during download, so no microdata were available locally for computation. Because I could not verify a usable public microdata source in this run that jointly measured the exposure and outcome of interest, I did not fabricate estimates or run unsupported models. I saved a limitations log as an artifact.
research_question: Among women younger than 50 years, what is the prevalence of e-cigarette use, and is e-cigarette use associated with a history of epilepsy or seizures in available public data?

sources.label: CDC 2015 BRFSS Survey Data and Documentation page
sources.notes: Official landing page listing 2015 BRFSS overview, codebook, and data-file links; inspected to confirm dataset structure and file availability.

sources.label: CDC BRFSS 2015 Codebook Report PDF
sources.notes: Official codebook inspected by text search for e-cigarette, vaping, epilepsy, and seizure terms; no matching variables were surfaced in the accessible combined-file codebook view used here.

sources.label: CDC BRFSS 2015 SAS Transport archive
sources.notes: Attempted direct retrieval in Python for local analysis; download failed in this run because of container network resolution failure.

title: Feasibility assessment of estimating e-cigarette prevalence and its association with epilepsy among women under age 50
